title: Civil service reform
summary_en: Civil-service reform is a classic topic of political economy: should bureaucrats be protected (tenured) or political appointees? This paper builds a model in which bureaucrats and politicians interact, and analyses when civil-service protection is socially beneficial. The mechanism: tenure lets bureaucrats develop expertise, but reduces politician control. The trade-off depends on policy volatility, information asymmetries, and voter-verification of outcomes. The paper provides formal conditions under which civil-service reform improves government efficiency.
policy_relevance: Highly relevant to ongoing Hungarian civil-service reform debates (common theme of the Ujhelyi-Forand research agenda). The paper supports civil-service tenure in stable-policy environments but allows for political appointments in volatile-policy contexts. A technical foundation for Hungarian discussions about tenure protection and political-appointment scope. Caveat: theoretical; concrete Hungarian policy implications depend on parameter values that would need empirical estimation.
